comments:
Summary and contributions:
This paper proposes a method to produce context-sensitive token embeddings by grounding word types in WordNet synsets and hypernyms and computing a context-dependent distribution over those synset embeddings (using a sense prior and an attention-like MLP). The expected synset embedding is used as the token embedding, which is then fed into a bi-LSTM model for prepositional phrase (PP) attachment. The model (OntoLSTM-PP) is trained end-to-end; synset embeddings are initialized with AutoExtend on GloVe and fine-tuned. Experiments on the Belinkov et al. PP-attachment dataset show consistent improvements over type-level baselines (GloVe and GloVe-retrofitted) and outperform a prior strong system (HPCD full). The paper includes ablations (removing sense priors, removing context sensitivity), analysis over training data size, qualitative examples, and integration of PP predictions into a dependency parser.

Strengths:
- A clear, well-motivated idea: using an ontology (WordNet) to produce context-sensitive token embeddings via a learned distribution over synsets and hypernyms.
- End-to-end training of synset embeddings together with the downstream task is appealing and practically useful.
- Experimental evaluation is thorough for the chosen task: compares to reasonable baselines, includes ablations showing the importance of both the sense prior and the context-dependent attention, and shows how the method helps low-data or rare-word situations.
- The model outperforms a prior strong PP-attachment system despite not using some of the rich features that system used; this supports the practical value of the approach.
- Code availability is stated, which aids reproducibility.

Weaknesses and points for improvement:
- Scope of evaluation is narrow: all experiments are on a single task (PP attachment). The technique is generally applicable to other tasks but empirical validation beyond PP attachment would strengthen claims about general usefulness.
- Comparisons to other multisense or ontology-aware representation methods are limited. The paper compares to retrofitting and discusses related work, but a direct empirical comparison to other multisense embedding approaches (e.g., those that produce multiple vectors per type) would help position the work more clearly.
- Some quantitative inconsistencies in the text: the abstract and body mention slightly different absolute gains (e.g., 4.9% vs 5.4% absolute improvement). These should be reconciled for clarity.
- Practical/runtime and memory costs are not discussed in detail. The implementation uses fixed S and H per batch (with padding/truncation). Some indication of computational overhead relative to a standard LSTM-PP (both in training time and model size) would be useful for practitioners.
- The method assumes availability of a good ontology and exploits WordNet’s sense ordering. For tasks or languages lacking such resources, applicability is reduced; the authors note this but the paper would benefit from more explicit discussion of limitations and possible mitigations.

Minor comments / suggestions:
- Provide exact numbers and reconcile the small inconsistencies in reported gains (4.9% vs 5.4%) across the text/tables.
- Report coverage statistics: fraction of tokens/types present in WordNet in the dataset, and how many synsets are pruned by POS filtering—this helps readers judge how much the ontology contributes.
- Include runtime/memory comparison to the LSTM-PP baseline (e.g., training time per epoch, parameter counts for embedding layers) to quantify costs of the ontology-aware approach.
- Consider evaluating on at least one additional downstream task (or a held-out PP-attachment variant) to demonstrate generality, or include results with different ontology depths to show robustness.

Overall assessment:
This is a well-written, solid paper that combines ontology information with neural attention-style selection to create context-sensitive token embeddings. The modeling choices are sensible, the ablations are informative, and the empirical gains on PP attachment and in parser integration are convincing. The work is a useful contribution to methods that integrate lexical resources with distributional models. I recommend acceptance and would favor an oral presentation.
